title: 2025/08/21 - TI
displayTitle: 2025-08-20 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector
topic: Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector
description: 21 August 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector NZ Cargo Owners Council Jo Murray, Executive Officer Jade Elstone, Deputy Chair Brent Falvey, Chair Ia Ara Aotearoa Transporting New Zealand Dom Kalasih, Chief Executive Billy Clemens, Head of Policy and Advocacy
descriptionSummary: Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector. NZ Cargo Owners Council. Jo Murray, Executive Officer Jade Elstone, Deputy Chair Brent Falvey, Chair

chunks.text: We're looking forward to this. The inquiry with this is early days. Yeah. We've had, uh, one briefing so far from officials. Um, I've, I've personally visited almost every report and talked with every port. Um, but we will, we've, we've had some port visits, um, so far, and we will do some more. Uh, and we've got an extensive range of submitters and we've been very, very gratified by the interests that there is in this.

And, um, we hope that we can work our way through this and end up with some recommendations which might help the boat go faster, or in some cases even get into port or out of port and get into the right port outta the right port in an efficient way. So, um, anyway, what we'll do is we, we look forward to hearing from you and, um, uh, in your hands and then look forward to some questions.

We've got 45 minutes and then, um, we, we'll look forward to making most of that. Alright. So, thank You. Thank you, thank you. And thank you for the opportunity. Uh, we, we seriously welcome this inquiry, particularly given the importance of shipping to, uh, new Zealand's economic growth. So my name is Joanna Murray and I'm the executive officer of the New Zealand Cargo Owners Council.

And here with me today is our chair, Brent Falvey from ANCO Foods based in and, uh, Cotty Jade Elston from, uh, PPAC Forest Products based in Napier, both of whom have traveled some distance today to be here. Um, so just to recap quickly, so the Cargo Owners Council is an organization for large volume exporters and importers, uh, collectively.

Membership currently accounts for around 70% of containerized exports and a significant volume of import, uh, bulk exports, imports, and domestic product. So these are the guys with the skin in the game and have, uh, considerable insight into what's working and what isn't working across the shipping supply chain.

Um, although they come from diverse businesses, uh, the common thread for our members is a focus on advocating for re reliable, resilient, and efficient freight supply chain in order to minimize the cost of getting our exports into international markets and to minimize the cost of landing our input for New Zealand consumers.

Now we know that global shocks to the, to the supply chain are just ongoing, whether it be weather, uh, geopolitical tension, pandemics, those things are outside our control and we know they will continue. So our focus is to ensure that those things that are within the control of NZ Inc are managed to the absolute best level.

What, what we are looking for is peak performance across, uh, supply chain infrastructure across the costs, across, um, resilient ports. What we are really looking for is to run the smartest operation possible in our own supply chain, so that we are in the best possible position to mitigate big global supply chain shocks.

Now, you will have seen in our submission that we've painted a picture of how the New Zealand supply chain is currently far from efficient. Uh, it's been degraded by poor port productivity, by lack of intermodal connectivity and lack of coordinated infrastructure investment. We did cover a lot of ground in our submission at a fairly high level, and we're happy to talk to any of the aspects in our submission.

Um, and we would really welcome, welcome your questions today, what Brett and Jay are specifically going to talk about is, is how that core productivity in the supply chain affects their businesses, how it affects the New Zealand economy, how it is actually gonna stymie government's economic growth ambition unless something changes.

And we also, and we want to specifically focus in an area where we think there's a role for government. So, um, more than happy to take questions as we go through. In fact, we'd really welcome questions to make sure that we are talking to an area that you know, you're interested in. And if we are speaking in a language that you don't particularly understand, please stop us and get us to, uh, D Tech.

Um, and at the end, um, if we haven't covered an area that was covered in our submission and you'd like some detail on, we're also really happy to talk to that. Okay. Look, there'll be plenty of questions. I think let's get, let's get what you want to say as an initial intro first. Yeah. Um, take, take the submission as read. Um, but anything you wanted to highlight, then there will plenty to questions.

Excellent. Okay. So I'll hand over to Jay. Okay. At this point. Thank you. Good morning. Uh, so I'll start by recapping the port productivity. That was in our submission, but we have been able to update it with the quarter 2 20 25 data that we get from the MOT website. The fig data, which is on the screen.

On that screen, yes. Mine down here. Yeah, We've got screens all over. Oh, good. Because you've got all Over the place. Yep. So there's multiple ways of measuring port productivity, and this is just one of them, which is the crane rate. So the, the crane rate is the number of containers that are moved on and off the vessel by one crane in one hour.

And as you can see in the graph, where we currently are operating is at a level that was similar to 2012, 2013. So where we are currently operating is also 30% down from the peaks that we can see through early prior pre COVID as a We do acknowledge that some of those peaks may not necessarily be sustainable.

They did lead to some health and safety issues, but we don't think the answer is to be running at 70%. If we click to the next graph Please. So the second graph shows the vessel exchanges. So this is the number of containers that are moved on and off the vessel within one port call.

So from 2012 you can see that on average the vessel exchange has increased by 50%. The reasons behind this increase in exchange is generally bigger vessels or bigger ships that come in. There is some freight consolidation behind it as well.

But the bigger picture that is not being seen is that you would expect with bigger vessels, longer runs, less stopping and starting that there should be an increase in productivity. And we are not seeing that. We're not seeing that at all, and it's actually substantially declining. What this has resulted from, or what this has resulted is that in New Zealand we are falling behind our global competitors.

So we're falling behind on pricing, falling behind on efficiency. And we are certainly falling behind on reliability. Reliability. These are all very vital considerations in a commodity market. And I know there is so much good work happening in the coalface and in the manufacturing offices of out in wider New Zealand, and it's coming to a crunch at this point.

I can use my own day job at Ppac as a ex example of our economic impact. So since 2018, we are now paying 30% more to get our freight to Asia. We are paying 50 to 60% more to get our freight to India. And this government has a plan to move into India and that should be a concern.

These increases in costs are directly related to inefficiency in the sector. We have seen very little improvement or reasoning to substantiate the increase in these costs. The impact at our business level is that we are losing competitiveness if in fact we probably have already lost competitiveness.

So we are competing with Europeans and they have considerably reduced freight in comparison. In our example in Ppac, we're a big Cox Bay company. We are six to 7% of the local GDP. We employ close to a thousand people directly and through contracting we have some fantastic resources and we are doing our utmost to build and value add for New Zealand.

But We are getting it eaten away by the inefficient supply chain. We are not an isolated case. This is not just peck. This has been replicated in many companies throughout New Zealand. You could talk, talk about. Okay. Um, morning everybody.

Um, my, my day job as well as SCO Foods, which is a meat producer. We, um, coed office based in South island, but we have plants, New Zealand wine. Um, we've also been impacted by it. Our our freight bill before COVID, uh, was in the 20 millions and now it's in the forties. Um, that cost just gets pushed into the price of goods, whether that's local or, or international.

So it lifts all the prices up. Um, we, we can't sustain the cost within our margins, so therefore it just goes into the cost of goods. So you see the impact of that. You know, you might see a leg of lamb in the supermarket is now $80, but that's got freight costs inside there as well. Which, which large. But, um, what I wanted want to talk about as well was, um, in our submission, uh, we argued about the emergence of the, uh, um, hub and spoke model.

We think that's inevitable. That'll happen. Um, I'm not sure everybody understands what the hub and spoke model is, but, um, a hub would be a big international DC port and, and the spoke would be the smaller ports featuring to those hubs. Um, the feeders, the feeders would either be, would be a number of things. It would be, um, coastal shipping.

Uh, it would have to work very closely to rail into road transport. I think. Um, I think when we think about rail, there's been some great work done behind the scenes with rail. Uh, in actual fact what we've seen is, um, they've actually rebuilt their model and I was lucky enough to see their facilities and see the work that they've done. But for them to be fully successful for New Zealand, which is just for a benefit, which, which facility?

Uh, the one in Christchurch, ah, that's the one we were hoping to see like when we visited last week. Really great. Um, electric trains. Um, really, really good stuff, what they've done there. And but for them to be truly successful, they need volume and, and to get volume in the scale of economies, um, once you'll get efficiencies and then from efficiencies you also get gonna see cost reductions.

Um, so the hub and spoke model, working with that model with the, with the rail would, would be massive benefits for New Zealand. Massive. Also gotta work with, um, of course road transport guys. And, and also, uh, there has to be an ability for coastal shipping, uh, doing that. Um, but in readiness for the, for the hub and spoke model, um, it's critical that New Zealand freight supply chain builds efficiencies and we need an end to end multimodal, uh, coordination across the entire, um, supply chain.

Um, we can't, the supply chain, as you all know, is very complex. Uh, it goes from the farm gate or from the forest, um, to, to our ports in containers or, or bulk. Uh, but of course the imports is reverse so it goes back there. So it's enticed, um, supply chain, not just just focusing on a port.

Um, but currently there's no end to end coordination of the total supply chain whi which, which we really need. Um, and the supply chains only as efficient as the weakest link will allow. Uh, and focusing on one part of the supply chain, it neglects independence of the entire freight supply chain.

Um, what we must address urgently is that, um, because of this lack of coordinated investment in critical infrastructure, we need to get a much more strategic view about our freight flows from end to end. Uh, we need an efficient right size connections between ports, inland ports, road rail, and coastal shipping.

So our submission, there is no strategic lens over the freight supply chain at all at the moment. Sorry, Could you repeat that? There's no, there is no strategic lens over the freight supply chain. So a strategy needs to be based on quality data and it needs to be data driven. We see this as an easy win for the government in this area.

The infrastructure needs to be right timed, right sized, and the right requirements to meet the needs. It should go without saying that we need to avoid premature, unnecessary and duplicate spending. Any suboptimal spend in this area will be carried by us as cargo owners, but it will affect everyday New Zealanders, whether it be the cost of rising goods or whether it be the reduced profitability of the importers and exporters that will in end in the end, that will impact on the economy.

So New Zealand can only manage what is measured and there is no quality data in this space at the moment. Even the figs data, which is probably the best that we've got, the ports have their own variations of the calculations that they upload. That's, That is MOT data. Yep.

There needs to be a, a much wider and well-informed understanding of freight. We need to know where it goes, how much of it goes, where it moves, how it moves. The government needs this data to be able to make future plans in the transport area, but also the supply chain operators need this data and information so that we can make informed investment decisions.

It's all about the right place at the right time, not only for investment but for our freight. So as Brent alluded, the supply chains are really complex. So any decisions around supply chain need good knowledge and they need the understanding of the connections and the dependencies. The best source of their information is the people that deal with it day in, day out.

So that's us as cargo owners, that's transport operators, that's the ports and the rail. Uh, uh, as the council, we advocate for a freight advisory steering committee. We see government's role in this as bringing together the people, the data and the lived experience.

So manage the repository of that. But we see as a minimum that we need to collect data on how and where freight moves around the country. Mm-hmm. The truck turnaround times at ports and at key nodes. We need to identify the key freight corridors, be it on road, be it rail, or be it the inland port connections.

We need to forecast future movements and the potential impacts they may have on existing capacity and the efficiency and all of that should help to identify which parts of the network are being constrained. This will enable the right sized investment at the right time.

So the council advocates for this data to be at a national level so that the total cost and economic benefit can be defined based on quality New Zealand and New Zealand Inc. Data rather than at a local isolated level. There you go. Um, in conclusion, we want to support the government to set the clear strategy for freight.

We as cargo owners are the ones with the highest alignment to the country's economic interests. We need to be fully consulted and involved. We offer our IP to assist the task of planning for current and future freight. Our vision for New Zealand Inc is to be supported by an efficient, cost effective shipping supply chain where it enables our businesses to trade competitively, grow our export values, drive, drive job creations, and provide economic resilience.

We want to see government recognize that freight infrastructure and the associated supply chain components are not merely operational, that they underpin economic security and resilience. They need to, these need to be embedded in the transport and maritime planning and deserve a rigorous strategic approach led by quality data. Thank you. That's a really good, that's a really great start.

And can we have the, the we, we've got the, got there, we've got the presentation, but the notes were useful 'cause there are some words in there, which I think would very helpful. Mm-hmm. Sure. Okay. That would be great. I, we'll start with the questions, Carlos, and then I'll put myself on. Thank you for coming. Um, I just want to look a little bit more information on this graph. So the first thing I, there two questions.

The first one is, compared to the international, uh, uh, global, global other port in, uh, in, in other country, do you know what is the lift per hours for them? Because you mentioned we are less efficient. So how, what's the comparison? It is difficult to measure that because our ports are quite small compared to the international ports.

So however, there is a global index and it, I think it's referred to in our submission that we, uh, the New Zealand ports are in the two forties out of 300 odd ports. Yeah. It's actually worse than, I don't have the numbers. Yeah. It's Actually worse than that. We're in the last 50 out of 400 ports ranked. So in terms of, you know, the actual performance metric.

Alright. My, my second question is, um, by, by looking at the graph, we can see that things goes wrong at 2020 in quarter two for Auckland and Targa port in 2022 quarter four for Targa and Napier. Do you know what's happened with that? Sharp reduce in efficiency?

Uh, the can to bring up, There's a sharp decrease in in those. So that's COVID and also that, Yeah, so 20 20 20 was COVID 2020 Is COVID. Yeah. But what happened to 2022? So we just didn't recover, we just didn't, whereas the rest of the world gradually built back up.

That's one of the observations that we've made. New Zealand has just not really picked up post COVID. So it's not like there's some technical issues or some policy changes or health and safety rules changes. It's not due or this is it. There's, I'd have to double check the timing. There is automation. So they did stop their automation in Auckland.

So that didn't go to plan for them. So that did have an impact. But in general terms, we just have not picked up back with the rest of the world. Okay. As Measured by container. As measured by, Oh, uh, crane rate or, yeah, But it's the same for our bulk members.

It's the same across all cargo owners. Mm-hmm. I think one of the points that we brought out in our submission was that if things go bad at Auckland, IE if there are delays in Auckland, it is a ripple effect right through the rest of the supply chain. So hotel will be affected by slow moves in or delays in at port of Auckland.

Yes. And that's a, that's actually a regular theme. I, I put myself on lot of Dan then Because this is why I try to emphasize Karen, because in when you say that is due to the ripple effect of Auckland, but Auckland doesn't have anything changes in, in the sector of, you know, quarter fourth 2022, only Tower and Napier, they got a sharp drop and efficiency.

So this is why I'm trying to ask what causing this sharp, um, job and and efficiency for those two ports. I think it is part of the wider issue. There's not good data because I've a look At Ran some of your Like a valid point. You, Let's try not to jump in. Um, okay. Done Carlos.

Oh. So we just don't, don't know the reason why. Not specific, not specifically it as part of the general trends reason. Alright, thank you. So are, so let's, let's ask that question. Um, 'cause the Auckland bidders, we know that, I think it's about 50% of, of ships actually do things on time in New Zealand. Some of that's international, some of that's that's in New Zealand.

Um, but in terms of those, those efficiency reductions, those are quite stark. And you've got no idea, I mean, you've had conversations with ports, no idea what's causing it. One of the, one of the things would seem to be that they, they're having difficulty actually having enough space to manage the containers and then they go like, we've got the containers, which we wanna get out now the bottom of the stack of three or four of them and just the time to move those things around is inordinately inefficient.

Their ability to get space I think is challenged as well. But, um, you know, it was nothing at All. Space, space, space is an issue. Of course it is. Yeah. I like Littleton trying to fix that now. Mm-hmm. Um, also what was happening too is that, um, with the ships coming, they'd arrive at Auckland, well, whatever port generally Auckland first port because of the imports.

Uh, and they'd arrive off windows because of various delays. And so I dunno if you understand what a window is, but all the short port ships are guaranteed a certain time on a ber on a particular day at a particular tire. Yeah. So if they come in late, then they simply have to go in a queue. Mm-hmm. And then there can be, um, a number of ships sitting in the queue and then they slowly go through, um, Sorry. Oh no, I'll, no, no, no. I Was just gonna ask. I'll, I'll, I'll get my lit, my my order here.

Sorry about that. Um, and so, so they simply can't get through. And then if the port's all fully congested, it just keeps moving itself around the country. Um, and, and what also happens is that some of the ports, if like our large, our largest import port, um, have eight cranes, but really, really do see more than four working. Often you drive down the port and there's a ship on ber with one crane working.

Um, so you just see the efficiencies, just, I don't know. Well, we've been told by the CEO that, um, his focus is health and safety, not productivity. Um, I suspect that they haven't got Craig Manning or, or even enough equipment to do that, but I'd need to have a conversation with him.

Um, and if you think about the cost to that, ships can be to charter a ship for a day can be a hundred thousand dollars. If it sits off a port for a week and we've got nine ports and we've got numerous ships sitting around the coast, the numbers become hundreds of millions very quickly, which gets passed down to us, gets passed down to, to Kiwis.

We will be, we will obviously be talking to, um, to All Dan. Thanks Mr. Chair. Um, yeah, really interesting presentation and thanks for the data. I think that's really useful. Um, just to drill down to the port level, 'cause I think there's, clearly there's something going on across all of our port. So the productivity is across the country.

Um, we've talked, we've talked about space, um, we've talked about, um, data, lack data. Um, what can you tell me about skills and kind of the investment in the infrastructure? Like what are other ports doing across the world in terms of technology, infrastructure, investment, and then also on the skills front, I mean, um, you know, is what what would you say is the biggest driver of that poor productivity at the poor level?

I, I would say it's, it's probably comes down to many and number of skill workers that they've got. Automation is a big thing for the future. If you look at Singapore, they're just on a different planet compared to us. Um, there has to be automation to some degree. Um, unfortunately the automation that Auckland did didn't work for them. I, I, I believe they use the wrong type.

But there's automation all over the world that's very successful. I was at LPC just a few weeks ago. They were showing me their new plans, what they wanna do. They're gonna do automated stackers, which mean they can bring it off the ship, stack them up fire efficiently about people getting involved from health and safety point of view. And then they can reorganize that and put them back on the ships more efficiently. Automation's gonna play a big key going forward.

And are you sensing when you interact with some of our ports that they're, they're actively looking at this? Or are they sort of in They would love to do it. We just don't have money. I know Littleton and Tara are actively looking at it. Yeah. Um, I haven't spoken to NAP about automation. Their biggest problem at the moment. They're pretty Keen too, I think. Beg your pardon? I think they're pretty Keen too. Yeah. Are they? Yeah. Nap PPI's biggest problem is weather.

And so they've been really knocked around by weather over the last year or so. Um, so what they're trying to do now is they've just, uh, invested, I think with Dunedin, uh, with the dredge. And so they're gonna try to make the channel deeper so when the ships come in, if there's a big swell, they'll still be able to get in. So they're trying to figure out ways that they can improve. But they, they set a lot of ships set outside the last 12 months from Nate, but just due to swell.

Yes. And then on the manning front, I mean, uh, where are our massive, where are the biggest skill shortages in the sector? I would say Auckland's got, without being the CEO there, I'd say Auckland's quite obvious that they haven't got enough manning just 'cause, you know, you see one ship, one crane working, the question is why, you know, when they've got eight, you know, if they had four cranes working, gonna ship it would get out 75% faster.

Yes. And so, you know, as I say, that impacts all around the coast, but I'd suggest they've all got landing issues. Okay. And then finally, um, just as Freight Advisory Council mean, what's your view of government's role in setting that up? Are you thinking that the government needs to kind of set this in statute so that it's a formal body?

Or is it, or is it industry led? I mean, what's your sense of how, what's, what do you want from government in terms of how to set the structure up for the, uh, the, you know, the coordination of the data and the what's Yeah, I think we might be a bit agnostic in terms of that.

And the, the sense that we have though is that there needs to be, right now there is nothing Right now, there's just a, a vacuum in terms of bringing together, um, this lens, a freight lens, um, across all the myriad, if you like, um, sorry, maritime functions in a way.

And we feel that the voice of those at the coalface, the cargo owners, the transport operators, uh, and the ports, there needs to be a forum of some form us. And we've caught it, a steering committee or advisory committee to enable that kind of the, the strategy that kind of, um, discussion and strategy to evolve.

Um, our thinking was it needs to be government led, but we hadn't necessarily thought it would be a legislative entity, but government led to give it the, um, momentum and funding essentially. I mean, our owners all have day jobs. Our members all have day jobs. Um, it's, it's, it's a resource issue really. Mm, thank You. Okay. Uh, It's, Could I just come back to your question?

I've actually worked out, it's the format of a graph. It's quarter 1 20 23. It's just the way that Excel's showing the lines. And it's Cyclone Gabriel, Right? Oh, that's, yeah. Okay. Right. Fair enough, fair enough. That makes sense enough, Simon. Oh, thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, look, um, your, your submission is very helpful that it outlines some significant problems with productivity, but you also make the point that ports have increased the number of the charges, uh, at power 30 of your submission vehicle booking system

charges at Auckland have increased 2000%, uh, in the last four years. There seems to be some kind of cash grab, uh, by Auckland Council through the Board of Auckland. Um, but there's been no commensurate, uh, increase in productivity. So, um, what do you think the committee should be thinking about, uh, when, when we hear about that problem?

Look, we have raised previously our thoughts around port ownership that we think sits behind some of this. Ultimately, uh, it comes back to a point that we have made, um, right up front that we believe ports are need to be viewed as economic strategic assets as opposed to source and revenue with their owners.

And there is a relationship between, um, charging regimes and dividends paid back to local government. However, uh, yeah, that's not an area that we, we, there isn't a clear path for government intervention there. So in terms of, uh, economic regulation, is there any, uh, is there an economic regulator that covers what ports can charge and can? Uh, There isn't.

I mean, we have looked to potentially a commerce commission, uh, taking a close look at VBS charges and that might be something that our colleagues behind us. Right. Well, rose, certainly, I mean, the dark reality is that any of these charges will find their way back to cargo owners ultimately. And, um, VBS is one of the obvious ones that that system simply prices have gone up and up and up and there is, you know, productivity hasn't.

So, Hmm. What are we paying for? So, just so supplementary on that, this chair, if it's okay. So just thinking about, um, the fact that these businesses are essentially monopolies in their location. Uh, you know, people talk about moving the Port of Auckland, but hey, at the moment there's just one port.

Um, do, do you think that it would make sense to link fees and charges and revenues back to investment and productivity? 'cause at the moment it just looks like, it just looks like a big vacuum cleaner being applied to the, uh, freight industry, freight sector.

Um, but there didn't seem to be a corresponding level of investment in port or, or, you know, access to port productivity. Well, certainly we would like to see more accountability for productivity. One of the things that we have been discussing as part of our kind of a overall strategy is monthly KPIs at a port level publish on port productivity and how that relates to their fee charging.

Okay. You know, what are we getting? What are the bang for bucks that we're getting? And that's, that sort of goes back to good quality data and none of that exists at the moment. Okay. That's a really good point, Mr. Chair. And just one further, one last, last one. Yeah. Just on the governance and ownership of ports, you did reflect that, um, many of these ports are in local government ownership.

Uh, do you think that ports have sufficient access to capital to deploy for the type of automation or productivity investments, uh, that they might want to make in an ideal world? You know, 'cause I, I assume, I mean, I've met a lot of the port operators and management like, uh, the chair has, and they all seem to be, uh, generally very competent, capable people.

Um, do you, do you think it would be helpful if maybe they weren't in local government ownership? I was gonna say there's probably two answers to that, because there is towering report that obviously is desperately in need of investing Yes. And has been for several years now. Mm-hmm. So that is not necessarily a investment a, a limit to their investment.

There is other reasons that are causing problems there. Yeah. However, there are other ports without the, through the rest of the country that yes, their, their ability to access funding is limited. Mm-hmm. Littleton is currently country, which is a good example. Now they're asked, they've done a bit quite a large study, they need 800 million to get to where they need to be.

Uh, which I don't know how much the councils can, can offer, but it seems like a big chunk to me and how they're gonna fund that. I don't know. But the investment needs to be made. They, They have, They have given us advice when we, we visited them, but yes, it does involve the council having a, playing a part. So, yeah. Yes. But I guess, um, the issue, I mean, they said to us that they had 600 million worth of assets and they need another 800 million to invest, but people are gonna have to pay for that.

And I think it comes back to the point that you've been making, which is they're high cost now, but if we make the capital investments to increase efficiency, we also still have to pay for those. That, that's correct. And so, yeah. It's, but I, I think the point about data is really well taken.

Um, yes. And, and I guess because of the relationship between the port and the landside infrastructure that's needed to get things in and out of the port Gonna Run, inevitably, government has to be involved and making these decisions, whether it's local or central.

Yes. Because, can you get the question, Julian? There's a limit to what you can do. Yeah. Well, I mean, you guys have asked lots of questions. Yeah, no, no. The freight demand study was meant to be refreshed, but last time was 2018. Mm-hmm. Yeah. Yeah. At least for data? Yeah, We, about the structure, but I think being able to contribute is the main thing. Yeah. Yeah. Um, last one. Yeah.
